epigraph.text: Test everything; hold fast what is good.
epigraph.quote: The best estimator depends on the criterion by which we judge it.

content.text: When multiple estimators exist, how do we choose the best one?

items: **Variance:** How spread out are the estimates?
**MSE:** Overall accuracy combining bias and variance
**Consistency:** Does it converge to truth as $n \to \infty$?
**Efficiency:** How does variance compare to the minimum possible?

content.title: Cramér-Rao Inequality
content.content:
For an unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of $\theta$:
\[
 \Var(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)}
\]
where $I(\theta) = \E\left[\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta} \ln f(X; \theta)\right)^2\right]$ 
is the **Fisher information**.
content.label: Theorem

content.text: An estimator achieving this bound is called **efficient**.

content.title: Relative Efficiency
content.content:
The **relative efficiency** of estimator 
$\hat{\theta}_1$ compared to $\hat{\theta}_2$ is:
\[
 \text{RE}(\hat{\theta}_1, \hat{\theta}_2) = \frac{\Var(\hat{\theta}_2)}{\Var(\hat{\theta}_1)}
\]

content.title: Sample Mean vs. Sample Median
content.problem:
For normal data, the sample mean is more efficient than the sample 
median for estimating $\mu$:
\[
 \text{RE}(\xbar, \text{median}) = \frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.57
\]
The mean is about 57\% more efficient.
